request.question: "Could I Leave You?" was a song in Follies, a musical concering what?

title: The Fabulous Palm Springs Follies
Follies
Ziegfeld Follies of 1936
Could I Leave You? (song)
Ziegfeld Follies (film)
Fox Movietone Follies of 1929
Rock Follies
Mark Rodenhauser
Glorifying the American Girl
Our Gang Follies of 1938
sentences: The Fabulous Palm Springs Follies was a Ziegfeld Follies style dance and musical review show that played at the historic Plaza Theatre in Palm Springs, California, United States, seasonally from November to mid-May.
The Follies was founded in 1990 by Riff Markowitz and Mary Jardin.
Impresario Markowitz also served as the show's managing director and emcee.
The show was often credited with helping to revitalize and maintain the downtown area by bringing in patrons from around the globe.
The Follies was unique in that it only featured performers 55 and older and holds Guinness World Records for this claim to fame.
It was the subject of a short documentary titled "" which was nominated at the 70th Academy Awards for Best Short Subject Documentary.
A segment that aired on Seattle television station KOMO-TV that featured the Follies received an Emmy in 1997.
The shows attracted approximately 170,000 attendees yearly.
On June 5, 2013, co-founders Markowitz and Jardin announced they would close the Follies on May 18, 2014.
Follies is a musical with music and lyrics by Stephen Sondheim and a book by James Goldman.

The story concerns a reunion in a crumbling Broadway theatre, scheduled for demolition, of the past performers of the "Weismann's Follies", a musical revue (based on the "Ziegfeld Follies"), that played in that theatre between the World Wars.
It focuses on two couples, Buddy and Sally Durant Plummer and Benjamin and Phyllis Rogers Stone, who are attending the reunion.
Sally and Phyllis were showgirls in the Follies.
Both couples are deeply unhappy with their marriages.

Buddy, a traveling salesman, is having an affair with a girl on the road; Sally is still as much in love with Ben as she was years ago; and Ben is so self-absorbed that Phyllis feels emotionally abandoned.
Several of the former showgirls perform their old numbers, sometimes accompanied by the ghosts of their former selves.
The musical numbers in the show have been interpreted as pastiches of the styles of the leading Broadway composers of the 1920s ands '30s, and sometimes as parodies of specific songs.
The Ziegfeld Follies of 1936 is a musical revue with lyrics by Ira Gershwin, music by Vernon Duke and sketches by Gershwin and David Freedman.
The Ziegfeld Follies were a series of revues presented from 1907 through 1931, 1934, 1936, 1943, and 1957.
"Could I Leave You?"
is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.

Ziegfeld Follies is a 1946 American musical comedy film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer and directed by Lemuel Ayers, Roy Del Ruth, Robert Lewis, Vincente Minnelli, Merrill Pye, George Sidney and Charles Walters.

It stars many of MGM leading talents, including Fred Astaire, Lucille Ball, Lucille Bremer, Fanny Brice (the only member of the ensemble who was a star of the original Follies), Judy Garland, Kathryn Grayson, Lena Horne, Gene Kelly, James Melton, Victor Moore, William Powell, Red Skelton, and Esther Williams.

Fox Movietone Follies of 1929, also known as Movietone Follies of 1929 and The William Fox Movietone Follies of 1929, was a black-and-white and color American musical film released by Fox Film Corporation.
Rock Follies, and its sequel, Rock Follies of '77, is a musical drama shown on British television in the 1970s.
The storyline followed the ups and downs of a fictional female rock band called the "Little Ladies" as they struggled for recognition and success.

The series starred Rula Lenska, Charlotte Cornwell and Julie Covington as the Little Ladies, with support from Emlyn Price, Beth Porter, Sue Jones-Davies, Bob Hoskins, Stephen Moore, Derek Thompson, Denis Lawson and Little Nell among others.
The series was made with a very low budget for Thames Television, with a style inspired by fringe theatre.
Mark Todd Rodenhauser (born June 1, 1961 in Elmhurst, Illinois) is a former American football center who played 13 seasons in the National Football League with seven different teams.
He played college football at Illinois State University.
He was selected by the Carolina Panthers in the 1995 NFL Expansion Draft.
Rodenhauser started his football career with the Chicago Bruisers of the Arena Football League.
He also made an appearance in the popular series Football Follies, in the 21st Century Follies DVDs, where he could snap a football a full length of a basketball court.
Glorifying the American Girl is a 1929 American Pre-Code, musical comedy film produced by Florenz Ziegfeld that highlights Ziegfeld Follies performers.
The last third of the film (which was filmed in early Technicolor) is basically a Follies production, with cameo appearances by Rudy Vallee, Helen Morgan, and Eddie Cantor.
Our Gang Follies of 1938 (later reissued as simply "Follies of 1938") is a 1937 American musical short subject, the 161st short subject entry in Hal Roach's "Our Gang" ("Little Rascals") series.
Directed by Gordon Douglas as a sequel to 1935's "Our Gang Follies of 1936", the two-reel short was released to theaters on December 18, 1937 by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: "Could I Leave You?" was a song in Follies, a musical concering what?
hop1_docs: Could I Leave You? (song) | "Could I Leave You?"  is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.

When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back | "When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back" is a song written by Steve Clark and Johnny MacRae.  The song -- a bittersweet reflection of a condemned inmate's life, looking back at all the bridges he burned and wished could be repaired -- was recorded by several country music artists, including Sam Neely, Bill Anderson and the band Confederate Railroad.
What You Leave Behind (album) | What You Leave Behind is the third studio album by the American country music band Ricochet.  It was released in 2000 on Columbia Records.

Give You What You Like | "Give You What You Like" is a song recorded by Canadian singer-songwriter Avril Lavigne from her self-titled fifth studio album released in 2013.  It was written by Lavigne with her husband Chad Kroeger and David Hodges.  The song has been praised by critics for expanding Lavigne's musical diversity and exposing her to new genres of music and is the sixth track on the album.  Musically, "Give You What You Like" is a folk-pop song with lyrics that describe combating loneliness through swapping sex.

What You Could've Won | "What You Could've Won" is a song by English indie rock band Milburn and featured on their debut album, "Well Well Well".  Released on 30 October 2006, it was the third single released from the album and charted at #66 in the UK.

What You Want (Evanescence song) | "What You Want" is a song by American rock band Evanescence.  It was released as the lead single from the band's third eponymous album on August 9, 2011 through Wind-up Records.  The song was written by Amy Lee, Terry Balsamo and Tim McCord and produced by Nick Raskulinecz.  Lee described "What You Want" as a departure from the band's previous sound and said she was inspired to write the song by several experiences she had with her fans.  Musically "What You Want" contains a variety of musical elements and it uses drums, guitars, synthesizers and a piano.  Lyrically, the song has a theme of freedom.

It Could Happen to You (song) | "It Could Happen to You" is a popular standard with music by Jimmy Van Heusen and lyrics by Johnny Burke.  The song was written in 1943 and was introduced by Dorothy Lamour in the Paramount musical comedy film, "And the Angels Sing" (1944).
hop1_titles: When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back
response.summary_1:
Header: Primary topic — the song "Could I Leave You?" and the musical Follies.

Facts quoted from the passages:
- Passage [1]: "\"Could I Leave You?\" is a song written by Stephen Sondheim"
- Passage [1]: for the "1971 musical \"Follies\""
- Passage [1]: for the character "Phyllis"

Missing / ambiguity:
- Missing fact: what the musical "Follies" is "concerning" (its subject, plot, or theme) — bridge entity: Follies (1971 musical). No description of the musical's subject appears in the provided passages.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. Fetch "Follies (musical)" (1971) — inspect lede/infobox and the "Plot" or "Synopsis" sections for what the musical concerns.
2. If needed, fetch "Follies (Sondheim musical)" or the Wikipedia disambiguation page "Follies" to confirm the 1971 Sondheim musical.
3. Search keywords: "Follies musical about", "Follies plot Phyllis", "Follies themes Stephen Sondheim".
hop2_docs: I'm Still Here (Follies song) | I'm Still Here is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies".

The Big Picture (Michael W. Smith album) | The Big Picture is the third album by contemporary Christian music artist Michael W. Smith, released in 1986.  The title comes from "Pursuit of the Dream," in which it is heard a few times, but also applies to the variety of themes for young people to learn about from the songs.  That track instructs on pursuing goals, and other themes include the folly of escapism ("Lamu"), sexual abstinence before marriage ("Old Enough to Know"), and maintaining a positive self image ("You're Alright").  Track 9.  "Tearing Down The Wall" opens with a reverse snippet of Amy Grant's "Emmanuel".

Folia | Folia (Portuguese), La Folía (Spanish), also folies d'Espagne (French), Follies of Spain (English) or Follia (Italian), is one of the oldest remembered European musical themes, or primary material, generally melodic, of a composition, on record.  The theme exists in two versions, referred to as early and late "folias", the earlier being faster.
Could I Leave You? (song) | "Could I Leave You?"  is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.
Mad About Opera | Mad About Opera (Italian: "Follie per l'opera" ) is a 1948 Italian musical comedy film directed by Mario Costa.

The Sinbad Show | The Sinbad Show was an American sitcom starring comedian Sinbad that premiered on September 16, 1993 on Fox.  The show's main plot is about a bachelor taking in two orphaned children.  Chuck Brown performed this sitcom's theme music.  It was canceled after one season on April 21, 1994, with two episodes left unaired.

Castlebourne folly | The Castlebourne folly in the parish of Belbroughton, Worcestershire, England, was built as a sham castle towards the end of the eighteenth century and is similar in age and design to Clent Castle another folly about 2.2 mi away in an adjacent parish of Clent.  In the middle nineteenth century a house, Castlebourne (originally Castle Bourne) was built in a Gothic castle style close to the folly and connected by a wall about 20 yd long and 5 yd high with an archway.  The folly, wall and house are designated as a Grade II listed building by English Heritage.
hop2_titles: The Big Picture (Michael W. Smith album)
response.summary_2: "Could I Leave You?" is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical Follies, for the character Phyllis. The provided passages do not state what the musical Follies is concerning.
